message.content:
I'm auditing whether a spec (fix_enhancement.md) was correctly implemented in the repo at C:\repos\oracle.

Verify these SPECIFIC claims about a dedicated "macro" model route/slot and its fallback pool. For each claim, report VERIFIED / PARTIAL / NOT DONE / WRONG with exact file:line evidence and quoted code.

CLAIM 1: A new auxiliary model slot `macro` exists (separate from the `general` slot), with an admin picker setting `default_macro_route`, a `requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs'` filter, and a "Copy job brief". Seeded to `google/gemini-2.5-flash`.
- Check files: packages/ai/src/routes/errors.ts, defaults.ts, auxiliary.ts, capability-requirements.ts; apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx; packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql; packages/db/src/seed.ts

CLAIM 2: The three workers (source-outline.ts, macro-relationship-extraction.ts, source-coverage-audit.ts under apps/workers/src/trigger/) now resolve their model via the `'macro'` route instead of `'general'` — including any attempt-logging slot. Grep for 'general' and 'macro' route usage in those three files.

CLAIM 3: A macro fallback pool `model_pool_macro = [gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-pro, gpt-4.1-mini]` is wired through the pool machinery so the slot is no longer single-pick. Check packages/ai/src/routes/defaults.ts, candidates.ts, migration 84, and seed.ts.

Report exactly what you find, with file paths and line numbers and short quoted snippets. Do not speculate — if a file or symbol doesn't exist, say so. Be concise but precise.

attachment.content:
- designflow-e2e-tester: Hybrid AI end-to-end tester for the DesignFlow app (designflow-frontend Angular UI + designflow-bff / designflow-backend / -tracking / -item-master / -data-syncing Node APIs). Drives the real app in a browser like a human would — logging in, clicking through every screen, filling forms with valid/invalid/edge-case data, hunting for broken behavior — AND writes durable Playwright regression tests into the existing e2e/ suites. Use this whenever the user wants to test changes, "test the app like a human", do exploratory/QA testing, find bugs in a flow, verify a feature end-to-end, smoke-test before a deploy, or grow E2E coverage. Trigger even when they don't say "Playwright" or "E2E" — phrases like "make sure my changes didn't break anything", "test all the screens", "click through the app", or "QA this" all apply.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll audit these claims systematically. Let me start by reading the spec and the relevant files.

content.content: fix_enhancement.md

1	/**
2	 * Oracle model setting keys.
3	 *
4	 * Runtime model selection comes from settings rows and approved model pools.
5	 * This file intentionally does not export hard-coded model defaults.
6	 */
7	
8	import type { OracleModelRole } from './types';
9	
10	/** Settings row keys for the three production route selections. */
11	export const ROUTE_SETTING_KEYS = {
12	  interview: 'default_interview_route',
13	  extraction: 'default_extraction_route',
14	  synthesis: 'default_synthesis_route',
15	} as const satisfies Record<OracleModelRole, string>;
16	
17	/**
18	 * Settings key for the general-purpose / utility model used for internal
19	 * one-off jobs that do not fit one of the three primary stages.
20	 */
21	export const GENERAL_PURPOSE_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY = 'default_general_purpose_route';
22	
23	/** Image-vision model used by document ingestion to transcribe image uploads. */
24	export const VISION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY = 'default_vision_route';
25	export const VISION_REASONING_EFFORT_SETTING_KEY = 'default_vision_reasoning_effort';
26	
27	/** Translation model used by the bilingual claim layer. */
28	export const TRANSLATION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY = 'default_translation_route';
29	
30	/**
31	 * Macro-understanding model used by the source-outline, macro-relationship, and
32	 * source-coverage-audit workers. Distinct from the general-purpose slot on
33	 * purpose: these workers emit deep nested JSON and MUST use a model with real
34	 * (strict) structured-output support. See AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md for why Qwen
35	 * (json_object only) hard-failed here.
36	 */
37	export const MACRO_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY = 'default_macro_route';
38	
39	/**
40	 * Reasoning effort settings keys, one per stage. Values are 'off' | 'low' |
41	 * 'medium' | 'high' (unified across providers; adapters translate).
42	 */
43	export const REASONING_EFFORT_SETTING_KEYS = {
44	  interview: 'default_interview_reasoning_effort',
45	  extraction: 'default_extraction_reasoning_effort',
46	  synthesis: 'default_synthesis_reasoning_effort',
47	} as const satisfies Record<OracleModelRole, string>;
48	
49	/** Legacy settings keys kept temporarily during R1 migration. Read-only. */
50	export const LEGACY_OPENROUTER_SETTING_KEYS = {
51	  interview: 'default_interview_model',
52	  extraction: 'default_extraction_model',
53	  synthesis: 'default_synthesis_model',
54	} as const satisfies Record<OracleModelRole, string>;
55	
56	/**
57	 * Per-stage admin model pools. Each row is a JSON string[] of "provider/modelId"
58	 * IDs that are approved candidates for that stage. Empty is now a configuration
59	 * error at runtime.
60	 */
61	export const MODEL_POOL_SETTING_KEYS = {
62	  interview: 'model_pool_interview',
63	  extraction: 'model_pool_extraction',
64	  synthesis: 'model_pool_synthesis',
65	} as const satisfies Record<OracleModelRole, string>;
66	
67	/**
68	 * Fallback pool for the macro slot. Unlike the other auxiliary slots (which are
69	 * single-pick), the macro layer gets an ordered approved chain so one malformed
70	 * structured-output response can't zero the whole holistic layer. Must be
71	 * non-empty and must contain the `default_macro_route` primary. See
72	 * AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md ERR-001.
73	 */
74	export const MODEL_POOL_MACRO_SETTING_KEY = 'model_pool_macro';
75	
76	/** Emergency capability-enforcement override. Defaults to true when unset. */
77	export const ENFORCE_MODEL_CAPABILITIES_SETTING_KEY = 'enforce_model_capabilities';
78

1	/**
2	 * Auxiliary model registry.
3	 *
4	 * Auxiliary models are admin-selectable model choices that are not one of the
5	 * three strict pipeline roles. They are explicit single-pick slots in this
6	 * pass: unset or unresolvable settings fail loudly instead of using a shipped
7	 * hidden default.
8	 */
9	
10	import {
11	  GENERAL_PURPOSE_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
12	  VISION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
13	  VISION_REASONING_EFFORT_SETTING_KEY,
14	  TRANSLATION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
15	  MACRO_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
16	} from './defaults';
17	
18	/**
19	 * A single model capability the picker can filter on. These names match the
20	 * capability columns on model_capabilities and the ModelInfo shape returned by
21	 * /api/admin/models.
22	 */
23	export type AuxiliaryCapabilityFilter =
24	  | 'vision'
25	  | 'thinking'
26	  | 'tools'
27	  | 'structuredOutputs'
28	  | 'promptCaching'
29	  | 'outputCap'
30	  | 'pdf';
31	
32	export interface AuxiliaryModelDef {
33	  /** Stable id used as the GUI `?stage=` param and the resolver lookup key. */
34	  id: string;
35	  /** Settings row holding the chosen route/model id. */
36	  routeSettingKey: string;
37	  /** Optional settings row for reasoning effort. Omit when effort is irrelevant. */
38	  reasoningEffortSettingKey?: string;
39	  /** Capability the picker filters the model list on. Omit for any model. */
40	  requiredCapability?: AuxiliaryCapabilityFilter;
41	  /** Short plain-text label. The GUI may render richer copy keyed by id. */
42	  label: string;
43	}
44	
45	export const VISION_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
46	  id: 'vision',
47	  routeSettingKey: VISION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
48	  reasoningEffortSettingKey: VISION_REASONING_EFFORT_SETTING_KEY,
49	  requiredCapability: 'vision',
50	  label: 'Image vision model',
51	};
52	
53	export const GENERAL_PURPOSE_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
54	  id: 'general',
55	  routeSettingKey: GENERAL_PURPOSE_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
56	  label: 'General-purpose model',
57	};
58	
59	export const TRANSLATION_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
60	  id: 'translation',
61	  routeSettingKey: TRANSLATION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
62	  label: 'Translation model',
63	};
64	
65	/**
66	 * Macro-understanding model. Drives the holistic layer: source outlines, macro
67	 * relationship extraction, and coverage audits. It is a SEPARATE slot from
68	 * extraction on purpose — extraction mines verbatim-quoted atomic claims, while
69	 * this model reasons over structure and emits deep nested JSON. The hard
70	 * capability filter is structured output; the job brief additionally warns that
71	 * STRICT json-schema (not just json_object) is required.
72	 */
73	export const MACRO_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
74	  id: 'macro',
75	  routeSettingKey: MACRO_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
76	  requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs',
77	  label: 'Macro understanding model',
78	};
79	
80	/** Order here is the order the auxiliary cards render in the admin settings page. */
81	export const AUXILIARY_MODELS: AuxiliaryModelDef[] = [
82	  VISION_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
83	  MACRO_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
84	  GENERAL_PURPOSE_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
85	  TRANSLATION_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
86	];
87	
88	export function getAuxiliaryModelDef(id: string): AuxiliaryModelDef | undefined {
89	  return AUXILIARY_MODELS.find((a) => a.id === id);
90	}
91	
92	/** Set of auxiliary ids, convenient for membership tests. */
93	export const AUXILIARY_MODEL_IDS: ReadonlySet<string> = new Set(
94	  AUXILIARY_MODELS.map((a) => a.id),
95	);
96

content.text: Now let me check the remaining files for the admin UI, migrations, and seed, plus the three workers.

content.content:
1	-- Macro-understanding model route.
2	--
3	-- The source-outline, macro-relationship-extraction, and source-coverage-audit
4	-- workers previously borrowed the `default_general_purpose_route` (utility)
5	-- slot. That slot pointed at qwen/qwen3.7-max, which only supports loose
6	-- `json_object` output (not strict JSON schema) and intermittently omits the
7	-- required top-level arrays, hard-failing the macro layer with
8	-- AllCandidatesFailedError (see AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md). The macro layer now has its
9	-- own explicit, admin-visible slot that requires strict structured output.
10	--
11	-- Default seeded to openai/gpt-4.1-mini. IMPORTANT: Gemini's responseJsonSchema
12	-- REJECTS these deeply-nested macro schemas with 400 "specified schema produces
13	-- a constraint too complex" (verified in prod 2026-07-03 — see AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md
14	-- ERR-001), so the primary must be an OpenAI strict-json-schema model. Gemini
15	-- stays in the fallback pool (it can handle the simpler source-outline schema).
16	-- Admins can change this at Admin -> Settings -> "Macro understanding model" with
17	-- no redeploy. Idempotent: never clobbers an admin's existing choice.
18	
19	BEGIN;
20	
21	INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
22	VALUES
23	  (
24	    'default_macro_route',
25	    '"openai/gpt-4.1-mini"'::jsonb,
26	    'Model for the macro/holistic layer (source outlines, macro relationship extraction, coverage audits). Requires strict structured-output support. Gemini rejects the nested schemas (400 too-complex); use an OpenAI strict-json-schema model.'
27	  )
28	ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;
29	
30	COMMIT;
31
packages\db\migrations\sql\84_macro_pool_and_lens_budgets.sql

73: auxiliary?: { id: string; requiredCapability?: AuxiliaryCapabilityFilter }; 110: macro: { 114: Drives the macro (holistic) layer of document 119: macro relationship extraction (infers the cross-claim 129: models; use “Copy job brief” for the full technical spec. 133: 'Model for the macro/holistic layer: source outlines, macro relationship extraction, and coverage audits. Requires strict structured-output support.', 468:const MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Macro Understanding Model" role brief 477: 2. macro-relationship-extraction — runs AFTER atomic claims exist. Reads the already-validated claims and proposes durable cross-claim relationships (dependency, handoff, sequence, exception_path, policy_vs_practice_tension, workaround_to_system_limitation, definition_resolution). Each relationship must be supported by ≥2 real claim IDs — its evidence is claim IDs, NOT free text. 478: 3. source-coverage-audit — runs AFTER. Compares the outline's expectations against the claims + relationships actually produced and emits "coverage findings" (missing stage, missing owner, missing branch, unresolved reference, unrepresented exception, low_claim_density, macro_only_source). Findings become gaps; they never assert new facts as true. 505:[Omitted long matching line] 527:[Omitted long matching line] 534: macro: MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF, 622: auxiliary: { id: def.id, requiredCapability: def.requiredCapability },

50:  { key: 'default_macro_route', value: 'openai/gpt-4.1-mini', description: 'Macro/holistic-layer model (source outlines, macro relationship extraction, coverage audits). content.text: Significant findings already: the seed and migration seed to `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`, NOT `google/gemini-2.5-flash` as CLAIM 1 and CLAIM 3 assert. Let me read migration 84 and verify the admin UI picker + brief, and confirm no lingering `'general'` route in the three workers.

content.content:
1	-- Macro fallback pool + coverage-first lens budgets (2026-07-03).
2	--
3	-- (1) model_pool_macro: the macro slot now has an ordered fallback chain so one
4	--     malformed structured-output response can't zero the holistic layer
5	--     (AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md ERR-001). Must contain the default_macro_route primary
6	--     (google/gemini-2.5-flash) and only strict-json-schema-capable models.
7	--
8	-- (2) Lens fan-out budgets: the old defaults (model_calls=4, groups=8) plus the
9	--     lens-major selection meant a 10-stage swimlane diagram ran ONE lens on the
10	--     first 4 stages and dropped the terminal stages entirely (fix_enhancement.md
11	--     §5 Bugs A/B/F). The selection is now coverage-first (every stage gets its
12	--     top lens before any stage gets a second), so the model-call ceiling must be
13	--     high enough to cover the stages. These are UPDATEs (not inserts) because the
14	--     rows already exist from migration 81; this is a deliberate behavior fix.
15	
16	BEGIN;
17	
18	-- OpenAI-first: Gemini rejects the nested macro relationship/coverage schemas
19	-- (400 too-complex), so the primary + top fallbacks are OpenAI strict-json-schema
20	-- models; gemini-2.5-pro stays as a cross-vendor tail (fine for outlines).
21	INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
22	VALUES
23	  (
24	    'model_pool_macro',
25	    '["openai/gpt-4.1-mini", "openai/gpt-4.1", "google/gemini-2.5-pro"]'::jsonb,
26	    'Ordered fallback chain for the macro slot (source outlines, macro relationships, coverage audits). Must include the default_macro_route primary and only strict-structured-output models. OpenAI first — Gemini rejects the nested schemas.'
27	  )
28	ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;
29	
30	-- Coverage-adequate lens fan-out ceilings. Cost stays bounded by the token
31	-- ceiling and the model-call cap; the selection covers stages breadth-first.
32	-- Guarded on the original migration-81 defaults so a re-run of this idempotent
33	-- file never clobbers an admin's later tuning.
34	UPDATE settings SET value = '16'::jsonb
35	  WHERE key = 'macro_max_lens_model_calls_per_document' AND value = '4'::jsonb;
36	UPDATE settings SET value = '20'::jsonb
37	  WHERE key = 'macro_max_lens_groups_per_document' AND value = '8'::jsonb;
38	UPDATE settings SET value = '64000'::jsonb
39	  WHERE key = 'macro_max_lens_estimated_input_tokens' AND value = '32000'::jsonb;
40	
41	COMMIT;
42
No matches found

460	- Long context window — one short claim per call.
461	- Streaming — consumed by a background worker, not rendered live.
462	- Batch API discount — volume is low and selective; not worth the added plumbing.
463	- PDF / audio / video — out of scope.
464	
465	BOTTOM LINE
466	Pick the model with the best, most natural translation quality for your exact language pair and the strictest "output only the translation" discipline — a Chinese-native flagship (e.g. Qwen-Max-tier) is a strong default for English<->Chinese. Ignore reasoning, tools, structured output, vision, and long context; keep reasoning effort low. Since it runs async on only the claims you direct to a language group, spend for fidelity, not speed or cost. Validate the final choice with a small A/B (this model vs one alternative) on ~15 real claims judged by a bilingual reviewer.`;
467	
468	const MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Macro Understanding Model" role brief
469	(Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.)
470	
471	PURPOSE
472	This model builds The Oracle's HOLISTIC understanding of a source. Atomic extraction (a different model) breaks a document into many small, quote-validated "claims." That is necessary but not sufficient: a swimlane diagram or SOP is a whole business process, not a bag of isolated arrows. This model's job is to understand and represent the BIG PICTURE — the end-to-end lifecycle, who owns each stage, the order and dependencies, the conditional branches, the approval loops, the systems of record, and what the source shows that the atomic claims failed to capture. If atomic extraction answers "what are the facts," this model answers "what is the process, and does our set of facts faithfully represent it."
473	
474	WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE (it runs both BEFORE and AFTER atomic extraction)
475	Three background (Trigger.dev) workers share this one model setting:
476	  1. source-outline — runs on/near ingestion. Reads the whole source (all chunks, and for images the transcription) and produces a provisional structured "outline": the source's purpose, process stages, actors/owners, systems, customers/licensors, geographies, term/acronym definitions, handoffs, exceptions, open questions, recommended extraction "lenses," and meaning-based groupings of the source. This is the BEFORE pass: its output is injected as non-quotable CONTEXT so atomic extraction understands the source's shape (referents, acronyms, branches) instead of extracting blind. The outline is GUIDANCE ONLY — never evidence, never quotable.
477	  2. macro-relationship-extraction — runs AFTER atomic claims exist. Reads the already-validated claims and proposes durable cross-claim relationships (dependency, handoff, sequence, exception_path, policy_vs_practice_tension, workaround_to_system_limitation, definition_resolution). Each relationship must be supported by ≥2 real claim IDs — its evidence is claim IDs, NOT free text.
478	  3. source-coverage-audit — runs AFTER. Compares the outline's expectations against the claims + relationships actually produced and emits "coverage findings" (missing stage, missing owner, missing branch, unresolved reference, unrepresented exception, low_claim_density, macro_only_source). Findings become gaps; they never assert new facts as true.
479	
480	WHAT IT IS FED (input)
481	- A large STRUCTURED-JSON system prompt + schema for whichever of the three tasks is running.
482	- For outlines: the full source text/topology (can be long — a whole document or diagram transcription), plus, for visual sources, the image itself when the model is multimodal.
483	- For relationships: a bounded set of candidate claim summaries with their IDs, types, "claim kinds," and statuses.
484	- For coverage: the outline JSON plus the extracted claims/relationships JSON.
485	It is NOT user-facing. Nobody is waiting on it. It runs asynchronously in the background at document scale.
486	
487	WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) — this is the crux
488	- DEEP, NESTED, SCHEMA-VALID JSON, every time. The output schemas are large and HEAVILY NESTED — arrays of objects that themselves contain arrays of objects, several levels deep (e.g. stages, each with arrays of inputs/outputs/next-stage IDs/source refs; relationships, each with an array of supporting-claim objects with roles; arrays of findings). The runtime validates every response against a strict schema and FAILS THE WHOLE TASK if any required field or nested array is missing or malformed.
489	- Faithful, conservative reasoning: say "unknown" when the source doesn't say; never invent stages, owners, or entities; only reference claim IDs / source refs from the supplied list; never introduce a named entity not present in the support claims or the entity registry.
490	- Genuine holistic comprehension of process structure: correctly reading a diagram's sequence, branches (new vs existing, pass vs fail), loops (revision/re-sampling), cross-swimlane handoffs, and terminal outcomes.
491	
492	PROCESS IT USES
493	Asynchronous, background, single-shot STRUCTURED-OUTPUT generation (runObject) per task. Long input, long nested output. No human latency requirement. It reasons over structure; it does not fetch anything itself (retrieval/claim-selection happens before the call).
494	
495	WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED
496	- STRICT, SCHEMA-ENFORCED structured output — the model is constrained to conform to a supplied JSON schema, rather than emitting best-effort JSON that is parsed afterward. This is the single most important attribute — see hard requirements.
497	- Strong multi-step reasoning over structure: assembling a lifecycle, detecting branches/loops/handoffs, spotting coverage gaps and policy-vs-practice tension. This is a synthesis/reasoning task, closer to the Synthesis role than to verbatim extraction.
498	- Large context window: outlines can ingest a whole document or a dense diagram transcription in one call (aim comfortably >100K, more is better).
499	- Generous max OUTPUT tokens: the nested JSON for a 10-stage workflow with branches is large; truncated output = schema failure.
500	- Multimodal (image) input is a strong PLUS for source outlines of diagrams/flowcharts/spec sheets, so the model can reconcile the picture with the parsed text.
501	- Reliability/determinism of schema adherence across retries. Cost/throughput matter (runs at document volume, async), but correctness of structured output dominates.
502	
503	CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements)
504	- STRICT, SCHEMA-ENFORCED structured output — NON-NEGOTIABLE. The model must be constrained to emit JSON that conforms to a supplied schema, not best-effort / free-form JSON. Best-effort JSON modes intermittently omit required fields or whole nested arrays; the runtime hard-validates every response and FAILS THE ENTIRE TASK on any missing required field, so best-effort JSON produces frequent, total failures.
505	- Ability to ACCEPT AND FULLY POPULATE DEEP, VERY COMPLEX, HEAVILY NESTED schemas specifically — not merely "has a JSON mode." Some models expose a schema/structured mode but REJECT schemas past a certain nesting depth or size (e.g. an error that the schema is too complex), or silently truncate or flatten them. The model must accept the entire deeply-nested schema and correctly populate every required level. This is a distinct, HIGHER bar than "supports structured output" and must be tested against the real macro schemas, not a small/toy schema.
506	- Large context window (>100K; bigger is better for whole-source outlines).
507	- High max-output-token ceiling for deep nested JSON.
508	- Strong instruction-following and reasoning.
509	
510	CAPABILITIES THAT ARE NICE TO HAVE
511	- Image/multimodal input (helps outline quality on visual sources).
512	- Reasoning/"thinking" controls — modest effort can help assemble structure; keep an eye on cost.
513	- Prompt caching for the stable schema/system prefix.
514	
515	CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED
516	- Low latency / streaming — it is a background job; nobody is watching.
517	- Tool / function calling — retrieval and claim selection happen before the call.
518	- Verbatim-quote fidelity — unlike atomic extraction, this model does not quote the source; its evidence is claim IDs and its outline is explicitly non-quotable.
519	
520	NEGATIVE ATTRIBUTES (avoid)
521	- Best-effort / free-form JSON only, with no real schema enforcement — the top cause of failure here.
522	- A schema/structured mode with a low complexity or nesting ceiling — anything that rejects, truncates, or flattens large deeply-nested schemas will fail these tasks even though it nominally "supports structured output."
523	- Small context window or low maximum-output-token cap — truncates the input or the nested output and fails validation.
524	- Tendency to hallucinate entities/stages, or to ignore "reference only the supplied IDs" — poisons provenance and gets rejected by the entity/support validators anyway.
525	
526	BOTTOM LINE
527	This slot needs a strong, long-context REASONING model whose structured output is STRICTLY SCHEMA-ENFORCED and that can accept and fully populate a DEEP, VERY COMPLEX, HEAVILY NESTED schema (test this against the real macro schemas, not a simple one), with a high maximum-output-token ceiling; multimodal input is a bonus for diagrams. Avoid best-effort-JSON-only models and any model whose schema mode caps out on nesting/complexity. Do not optimize for latency. This slot is intentionally separate from the Extraction model: extraction mines verbatim-quoted facts, while this model reasons about the whole process and must emit deep, nested, schema-valid JSON.`;
528	
529	// Clipboard "job brief" text by auxiliary-model id. Declared after the brief
530	// literals (which are large) so the earlier AUX_PRESENTATION map stays free of
531	// forward references. Aux models without a brief simply omit an entry.
532	const AUX_CLIPBOARD_BRIEFS: Record<string, string> = {
533	  vision: VISION_MODEL_BRIEF,
534	  macro: MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF,
535	  translation: TRANSLATION_MODEL_BRIEF,
536	};
537	
538	const STAGE_MODEL_ROLES: RoleDef[] = [
539	  {

content.text:
I now have complete evidence for all three claims.

# Audit Results: fix_enhancement.md — macro model route/slot

## CLAIM 1 — new `macro` slot + admin picker + capability filter + job brief + seeded model

**Verdict: PARTIAL** (fully implemented as a feature, but seeded model is WRONG vs. the claim)

Structurally the slot exists and is wired end-to-end:

- **Slot type** — `packages/ai/src/routes/errors.ts:3`: `export type ModelSlot = OracleModelRole | 'vision' | 'general' | 'translation' | 'macro';`
- **Setting key `default_macro_route`** — `packages/ai/src/routes/defaults.ts:37`: `export const MACRO_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY = 'default_macro_route';`
- **Auxiliary def, separate from general, with `requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs'`** — `packages/ai/src/routes/auxiliary.ts:73-78`:
  ```ts
  export const MACRO_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
    id: 'macro',
    routeSettingKey: MACRO_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
    requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs',
    label: 'Macro understanding model',
  };
  ```
  It is a distinct card from `GENERAL_PURPOSE_AUXILIARY_MODEL` (`auxiliary.ts:53-57`) and both are listed in `AUXILIARY_MODELS` (`auxiliary.ts:81-86`).
- **Capability requirement enforced at routing** — `packages/ai/src/routes/capability-requirements.ts:31-36` returns `[{ kind:'capability', field:'structuredOutputs', label:'structured outputs' }]` for `case 'macro'`.
- **Admin picker filters the model list on that capability** — `apps/web/app/admin/settings/_components/model-picker.tsx:312-317`: `const auxCap = auxiliary?.requiredCapability; ... auxCap ? models.filter((m) => m[auxCap]) : models`. Wired from `page.tsx:622`: `auxiliary: { id: def.id, requiredCapability: def.requiredCapability }`.
- **"Copy job brief"** — button exists (`model-picker.tsx:353-361`, label `'Copy job brief'` at :361, copies `clipboardBrief` at :172-174). The macro brief is `MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF` (`page.tsx:468-527`) mapped via `AUX_CLIPBOARD_BRIEFS.macro` (`page.tsx:534`) and passed as `clipboardBrief: AUX_CLIPBOARD_BRIEFS[def.id]` (`page.tsx:621`).

**WRONG part of the claim — the seeded model.** The claim says "Seeded to `google/gemini-2.5-flash`." It is **not**. Both the migration and the seed use `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`:
- `packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql:24-26`: `'default_macro_route', '"openai/gpt-4.1-mini"'::jsonb`
- `packages/db/src/seed.ts:50`: `{ key: 'default_macro_route', value: 'openai/gpt-4.1-mini', ... }`

The migration comment (`83_...sql:11-15`) explicitly explains why: Gemini's `responseJsonSchema` rejects the deeply-nested macro schemas with a 400 "constraint too complex" (verified in prod 2026-07-03, AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md ERR-001), so the primary must be an OpenAI strict-json-schema model. Gemini is relegated to the fallback tail only. So the claim's asserted seed value is factually contradicted by the code.

## CLAIM 2 — three workers resolve via `'macro'` route, not `'general'`

**Verdict: VERIFIED**

All three workers import and call `resolveRouteCandidates(db, 'macro')`, including their attempt-logging `slot:` fields. No `'general'` / `resolvePrimaryRouteFromSettings` / `GENERAL_PURPOSE` usage remains in any of the three (grep returned "No matches found").

- `source-outline.ts` — import at :20; `resolveRouteCandidates(db, 'macro')` at :376; `slot: 'macro'` at :453 and :498.
- `macro-relationship-extraction.ts` — import at :15; call at :308; `slot: 'macro'` at :390 and :429.
- `source-coverage-audit.ts` — import at :13; call at :111; `slot: 'macro'` at :180 and :219.

## CLAIM 3 — macro fallback pool wired through pool machinery

**Verdict: PARTIAL** (mechanism fully implemented; the claim's asserted pool contents are WRONG)

The pool machinery is genuinely wired:

- **Setting key** — `packages/ai/src/routes/defaults.ts:74`: `export const MODEL_POOL_MACRO_SETTING_KEY = 'model_pool_macro';`
- **Slot resolves a pool key only for macro** — `packages/ai/src/routes/candidates.ts:66-69`: `poolKey: slot === 'macro' ? MODEL_POOL_MACRO_SETTING_KEY : undefined`. Downstream this drives multi-candidate ordering (`candidates.ts:204-235`): the pool is read, must be non-empty (:208-210 throws if empty), the primary must be present in the pool (:227-232), and remaining pool entries become ordered fallbacks (:234-235). So the slot is no longer single-pick.
- **Migration 84 exists** — `packages/db/migrations/sql/84_macro_pool_and_lens_budgets.sql:24-27` inserts `model_pool_macro`.
- **Seed** — `packages/db/src/seed.ts:51`.

**WRONG part — the pool contents.** The claim states `model_pool_macro = [gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-pro, gpt-4.1-mini]`. Actual value in both migration and seed is a different, OpenAI-first list:
- `84_...sql:25`: `'["openai/gpt-4.1-mini", "openai/gpt-4.1", "google/gemini-2.5-pro"]'`
- `seed.ts:51`: `['openai/gpt-4.1-mini', 'openai/gpt-4.1', 'google/gemini-2.5-pro']`

`gemini-2.5-flash` is absent entirely; `gpt-4.1` is present (not in the claim); ordering is OpenAI-first, not Gemini-first.

## Note on internal inconsistency
Migration 84's header comment (`84_...sql:6`) still describes the primary as `google/gemini-2.5-flash` — a stale comment that contradicts the actual inserted value on line 25 (`openai/gpt-4.1-mini`) and the rest of the same file's rationale. Worth flagging but it does not affect runtime behavior.

## Summary

| Claim | Verdict | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — macro slot, picker, `structuredOutputs` filter, job brief | PARTIAL | All structure VERIFIED; seeded model is `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`, **not** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` as claimed (WRONG) |
| 2 — three workers use `'macro'` route | VERIFIED | All three call `resolveRouteCandidates(db, 'macro')`; zero `'general'` references remain |
| 3 — macro fallback pool wired | PARTIAL | Pool machinery VERIFIED; pool contents are `[gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-4.1, gemini-2.5-pro]`, **not** the claimed `[gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-pro, gpt-4.1-mini]` (WRONG) |

The spec's *feature* was implemented correctly and thoroughly, but the audit's stated model identities (both the seed default and the pool list) are wrong — the implementation deliberately made the macro slot **OpenAI-first** because Gemini rejects the nested macro schemas (AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md ERR-001), the opposite of what the claims assert.
